# **Berkeley’s 2014 Payroll Was Split Between Seasonal Workers and High-Cost Public Safety Roles**

A look at 2,217 City of Berkeley paychecks from 2014 shows a payroll split in two: a large, low paid seasonal workforce on one side, and a smaller group of six figure public safety earners on the other.

**Spreadsheets Link**  
[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1LD2q3xe6EgcpJlr5kheofQKQYyJck2ibDcuayD5DHKo/edit?usp=sharing](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1LD2q3xe6EgcpJlr5kheofQKQYyJck2ibDcuayD5DHKo/edit?usp=sharing) 

**Data Source**  
City of Berkeley, *City of Berkeley Employee Salaries – 2014*, City of Berkeley Open Data Portal (Socrata), dataset ID rphs-8dfw. Accessed July 5, 2026\. [https://data.cityofberkeley.info/Economic-Data/City-of-Berkeley-Employee-Salaries-2014/rphs-8dfw/about\_data](https://data.cityofberkeley.info/Economic-Data/City-of-Berkeley-Employee-Salaries-2014/rphs-8dfw/about_data)

# **Where the data comes from**

The file is a record of what the City of Berkeley paid every one of its 2,217 employees in calendar year 2014\. Each row is one person and lists their job title, base pay, overtime pay, other pay, the taxpayer cost of their benefits, and two totals.

It is important to separate where I downloaded the data from the place the numbers were actually generated. The original source is the City of Berkeley's own payroll system. Under the California Public Records Act, government employee names and compensation are public information, so this data has to be released on request. The particular copy I am using follows the exact column format published by Transparent California, a database that compiles California public payroll through public records requests and posts it in a searchable, downloadable form.

Transparent California is not a neutral government portal. It is a project of the Nevada Policy Research Institute, a free market think tank, launched together with the California Policy Center. Both organizations focus on government spending and pension costs. That does not make the numbers wrong, and the underlying records come straight from the city, but it does mean the data reaches me through an organization with a point of view about public pay. The way the data is framed, especially the emphasis on total compensation including benefits, reflects that lens.

A few specific integrity questions shaped how I read the file:

* Benefits are a cost to taxpayers, not cash the worker took home. Transparent California's own documentation says the benefits figure represents what the employer spent on health and retirement, not money deposited in anyone's bank account. So the headline "Total Pay and Benefits" number overstates what an employee actually earned. I treat base pay and take home pay separately from that combined figure.  
* The agencies self report. Transparent California relies on each city to hand over accurate, complete records, and notes that some agencies withhold names or details. I have no independent way to audit Berkeley's submission.  
* Timing. Values are annual, but some agencies report on a fiscal year and some on a calendar year, which can shift where a paycheck lands.

# **Data analysis**

The raw file needed three fixes before anything would calculate:

1. The pay columns imported as text. Every dollar figure carried a $ and comma separators, which Google Sheets can read as words instead of numbers. On import I set "Convert text to numbers" to Yes, then confirmed each pay column was right aligned. Where a column stayed as text, I used Find and replace to strip the $ and commas so the values became real numbers I could sum and average.  
2. Three columns carried no information. Year was 2014 for every row, Agency was Berkeley for every row, and Notes was empty for all 2,217 rows. I set them aside.  
3. Two rows were anomalies. One employee showed a negative total of about minus 1,200 dollars, most likely a payroll correction, and one showed a total of zero. I filtered both out of the aggregate math and note them here rather than hiding them. That leaves 2,215 clean records.

**Pivot Tables**

**Pivot 1: average pay by job title** 

Rows are Job Title. Values are a headcount (COUNTA of Employee Name), the AVERAGE of Total Pay and Benefits, and the SUM of Overtime Pay, sorted by average pay from high to low. This one answers "which jobs pay the most per person." The result is that police and fire roles fill the top of the list. Police lieutenants average about 304,000 dollars, police sergeants about 259,000, and police officers about 197,000, while the bottom is dominated by seasonal roles like Youth Enrollee Intern at roughly 2,000 dollars. The headcount column matters here because it lets you ignore one person executive titles and focus on real, common roles. Berkeley's highest per person pay is concentrated in public safety.

**Pivot 2: total cost to the city by job title** 

Rows are Job Title again, but the value is the SUM of Total Pay and Benefits, sorted high to low. This answers a different question: "which jobs consume the largest share of the payroll as a group." A job can have moderate individual pay and still dominate the budget if enough people hold it. Police Officer sits far above everything at about 25.6 million dollars, because there are 130 of them, followed by Firefighter near 8.4 million and Police Sergeant near 7.8 million. The difference from Pivot 1 is important: Pivot 1 is about the individual paycheck, Pivot 2 is about the total bill. Here it’s shown that public safety is not just the best paid, it is also the biggest line item on the city's payroll.

**Pivot 3: overtime by job title** 

Rows are Job Title, and the value is the SUM of Overtime Pay, sorted high to low. This isolates one specific slice of pay to answer "where does the city's overtime money actually go." Police Officer leads at about 1.71 million dollars in overtime alone, followed by Police Sergeant near 767,000 and Fire Apparatus Operator near 643,000, with dispatchers and solid waste workers also near the top. This is the pivot that supports the most interesting argument in the story, because heavy overtime usually points to understaffing or mandatory coverage rather than simple generosity. It’s clear that overtime is heavily concentrated in a few public safety roles.

# **Visuals**

### **The charts**

#### **Chart 1: How Berkeley spends its payroll dollar**

*![][image1]*

A bar chart fits because I am comparing a few distinct categories against one measure, total dollars. The finding is that base pay is the giant at about 117 million dollars, but benefits are a surprising second at about 58 million, nearly a third of everything the city spends on its people. Overtime and other pay are comparatively small at about 8 and 7 million. This chart backs up a caveat that is easy to state but hard to feel: the "total compensation" figures include a large slug of benefit costs that no employee ever sees as cash.

#### **Chart 2: Where the overtime piles up**

*![][image2]*

A scatter plot is the right choice because the question is about the relationship between two numbers, base pay and overtime, across individuals. Most dots lie flat along the bottom, since about two thirds of employees earned no overtime at all. The dots that climb up the right side tell the story: only about a third of staff got any overtime, but 135 of them collected more than 20,000 dollars of it, and 32 cleared 40,000 dollars in overtime alone, topping out near 81,000. Those heavy overtime dots all cluster in the mid to high base pay range, roughly 59,000 to 160,000 dollars, which is the police and fire zone. The visual shows instantly that overtime is not spread across the city but concentrated on a specific group of public safety workers, some of whom nearly double their pay through it.

# **Summary, limitations, and ethics**

This project set out to answer a simple question about a single year of one city's spending: who does Berkeley pay the most, and what actually makes up those paychecks. The answer is that the city's 2014 payroll is really two payrolls stacked on top of each other. On one side sits a large seasonal and part time workforce, hundreds of interns, playground leaders, camp staff, and aquatics workers, most earning only a few thousand dollars for the year. The single largest job title in the entire city is Youth Enrollee Intern, with 328 people averaging about 2,000 dollars. On the other side sits a smaller, well compensated core of police officers, firefighters, and their supervisors, several of whom cleared 300,000 dollars once benefits and overtime were counted. Every one of the ten highest paid individuals in the city holds a police title. Because these two groups get averaged together, the commonly cited "typical city salary" tells you almost nothing about either one. The median total compensation is about 88,900 dollars, but drop just that one intern title and it jumps to roughly 101,900. Police officers are also the biggest single line item on the whole payroll, costing the city about 25.6 million dollars as a group, simply because there are 130 of them. The single most useful thing this analysis produced is a warning against trusting the average.

The finding that public safety dominates the top of the payroll is real, but it needs context rather than outrage, and that is where the limitations matter most. First, the largest number attached to each worker, total pay and benefits, is not money the worker received. Benefits alone make up about 58 million dollars of the city's 190 million dollar payroll, close to a third of the total, and that figure is the taxpayer cost of health insurance and pensions, not cash in anyone's pocket. Reporting it as take home pay would misrepresent what employees earn, so I kept base pay, overtime, and benefits separate throughout. Second, high overtime is not automatically evidence of waste. The city paid about 8.26 million dollars in overtime, and it is heavily concentrated: only about a third of employees got any overtime at all, but 135 of them collected more than 20,000 dollars of it, 32 cleared 40,000, and one topped 81,000. That pattern in police and fire is often a symptom of understaffing, mandatory minimum staffing rules, or emergency coverage, and this dataset alone cannot tell the difference. Third, this is a single year. Without several years I cannot say whether 2014 was normal, high, or low, and I cannot adjust for inflation or the Bay Area cost of living, both of which would change how these numbers read today.

The ethical stakes here are concrete because every salary in this file is attached to a named individual. Public employee salaries are legally public, and that transparency serves a real purpose, but a searchable, name by name database can still expose ordinary workers to unwanted attention, particularly people in low level roles or sensitive positions. For that reason I built the entire analysis on aggregates. I grouped by job title and reported group averages and totals, and even the scatter plot shows individuals only as unlabeled dots, never as named paychecks held up for display. There is also a framing risk baked into the source. This data is from Transparent California, a project of the Nevada Policy Research Institute, a free market think tank with a stated interest in restraining government pay and pension costs. That does not make the numbers wrong, but it would be easy to slide into an "overpaid bureaucrats" narrative. I tried to resist that by giving equal weight to the large share of the workforce earning very little and by flagging understaffing as a plausible driver of the overtime figures. 

To turn this into a complete and responsible story, more reporting would be needed. I would cross check Berkeley's numbers against the California State Controller's official Government Compensation in California database to confirm Transparent California recorded them faithfully. I would interview the city's human resources office and the police and fire unions to understand why overtime runs so high and whether staffing gaps are the cause. I would compare Berkeley against a few peer cities on a per resident basis, and pull multiple years to see the trend rather than a single snapshot.
